citations: Crothers, George M. 2012. Early Woodland Ritual Use of Caves in Eastern North America. American Antiquity 77(3):524-541.
Homsey-Messer, Lara. 2015. Revisiting the Role of Caves and Rockshelters in the Archaic Midsouth. American Antiquity 80(2):332-352.
Watson, Patty Jo (ed.). 1974. Archaeology of the Mammoth Cave Area. Academic Press, New York.
Adovasio, J.M. et al. 1990. The Meadowcroft Rockshelter Radiocarbon Chronology. American Antiquity 55(2):348-354.

features.popup:
◉ Mammoth Cave — The Underworld

UNESCO World Heritage Site • National Park • Longest cave system in the world (~580 km)

Location: Edmonson County, west-central Kentucky (37.187°N, 86.101°W)

The Mammoth Cave System is the longest mapped cave in the world — currently ~580 km (360 mi) of surveyed passages. Prehistoric peoples explored the deep interior for over 5,000 years, beginning ~3650–2460 cal. B.C. The most intensive activity occurred during the Early Woodland period (~1200–200 cal. B.C.), when 44 of 53 radiocarbon dates cluster in this window. Exploration required portable light (river cane torches, weed stalks, woody branches) and extended miles from the natural entrance into total darkness.

The archaeological record (Crothers 2012): Over 6,000 m (19,700 ft) of passage have been systematically surveyed for archaeological remains over 12 years of fieldwork. More than 6,000 prehistoric artifacts and features documented. Three categories: (1) torch debris and lighting materials (58% of assemblage); (2) mineral collection tools — mussel shell scrapers, hammerstones, gourd and wooden containers, wood poles to scale walls (12%); (3) incidental remains — cordage, twined fabric, woven slippers, paleofecal specimens (30%). Paleofecal specimens comprise 24% of all artifacts — an extraordinary preservation context.

Male initiation ritual (Crothers 2012): Steroid analysis of 12 paleofecal specimens (6 from Mammoth, 6 from Salts Cave) showed all 12 had testosterone-to-estradiol ratios in the male range — deep-cave activity was exclusively male. Crothers argues the mining was part of communal male rites of passage: adolescent boys brought into adulthood through cave ordeals. Three lines of evidence converge: (1) medicinal salts (mirabilite, epsomite) ingested as ritual purgatives — saline laxatives that would intensify the ordeal; (2) evidence of altered states of consciousness from sensory deprivation, fatigue, and possible psychotropic substances (sweetflag/Acorus rhizome, possible maygrass beer); (3) gypsum collection as a symbolic marker — gypsum can only be obtained deep inside the cave, so possessing it proved completion of the initiation. Parallel: Hopi Wü'wütçim ceremony, where initiates collect white gypsiferous clay from a cave during their rite and use it as body decoration upon re-emergence.

Cave art: Charcoal pictographs found deep in the passages — lattice patterns, spirals, wavy lines, concentric circles, zigzags, and a few anthropomorphic and zoomorphic figures. These conform to the entoptic phenomena model (Lewis-Williams & Dowson 1988) — universal geometric forms generated by the human nervous system during altered states of consciousness. Only one location contains multiple anthropomorphic figures alongside geometric forms.

Preserved bodies: Two exsiccated (naturally desiccated) bodies found deep in the cave. (1) A ~45-year-old male discovered in 1935, crushed by a limestone block he had undermined while mining gypsum — wearing only a twined blanket. (2) A ~9-year-old boy found in Salts Cave in 1875 in fetal position, without clothing or blankets, with hemorrhaging behind the sternum suggesting a blow to the chest. Both found >2 km from the nearest entrance.

Today: Mammoth Cave National Park (NPS). UNESCO World Heritage Site (1981). Over 400 miles of mapped cave. Guided tours available. The prehistoric passages are not on the standard tourist routes.

Sources: Crothers 2012 (American Antiquity 77:524-541); Watson 1974; Kennedy 1996; Carstens 2001; Munson et al. 1989; NPS

◉ Salts Cave

Part of the Mammoth Cave System • Separate entrance 4.8 km from Mammoth

Location: Mammoth Cave National Park, Edmonson County, Kentucky (37.150°N, 86.060°W)

Although part of the same geological system as Mammoth Cave, Salts Cave has a separate natural entrance 4.8 km (3 mi) away and was used independently in prehistory. Each entrance was explored 6–8 km (3.7–5.0 mi) deep. The vestibule area shows typical entrance-zone occupation (hearths, refuse, burials), but deep passages contain the same mineral mining, torch debris, and paleofecal evidence as Mammoth Cave.

Key finds: The vestibule excavations recovered nearly 2,000 human bone fragments comingled with occupational debris — mortuary activity at the entrance appears unrelated to the deep-cave ritual use. The Salts Cave mummy (the ~9-year-old boy) was found in 1875 in a remote passage in fetal position. Paleofecal specimens from Salts Cave contain hickory nutshell, sunflower, marshelder (Iva annua), goosefoot (Chenopodium), maygrass, and occasional squash seeds — the eastern agricultural complex in its earliest form. Pollen analysis identified lily, iris, and sweetflag (Acorus americanus) in paleofecal specimens — insect-pollinated spring flowers that were either eaten directly or brewed as herbal tea, likely for ritual/medicinal purposes.

Sources: Crothers 2012; Watson 1969, 1974; Sobolik et al. 1996; Schoenwetter 1997; Bryant 1997

◉ Big Bone Cave, Tennessee

Early Woodland gypsum mining • Van Buren County

Location: Van Buren County, Tennessee (36.500°N, 85.650°W)

One of at least five documented Early Woodland gypsum mining cave sites in Kentucky and Tennessee beyond Mammoth/Salts. Contains evidence of mineral mining similar to that at Mammoth Cave, indicating the practice was more widely sought than previously known and may have had similar ritual uses on a regional scale (Crothers 2012). Named for the Pleistocene megafauna bones (giant ground sloth, Megalonyx jeffersonii) discovered there in the 19th century.

Sources: Crothers 1987, 2012

◉ Hubbards Cave, Tennessee

Early Woodland evidence • Warren County

Location: Warren County, Tennessee (36.285°N, 86.172°W)

Another cave with Early Woodland period evidence, cited by Crothers (Pritchard 2001) among the network of caves in Kentucky and Tennessee that show gypsum mining and similar ritual activity. Part of the broader pattern of cave ritual use that extended across the Interior Low Plateaus karst landscape.

Sources: Pritchard 2001; Crothers 2012

◉ Wyandotte Cave, Indiana

Epsomite mining • Crawford County

Location: Crawford County, Indiana (38.295°N, 86.218°W)

Wyandotte Cave may have been mined for epsomite (Munson and Munson 1990:71-73). Associated radiocarbon samples date slightly later than the Mammoth/Salts pattern (cal. A.D. 130–530 and cal. A.D. 130–770). No human paleofecal samples have been identified. The primary prehistoric activity was mining a large aragonite flowstone column and high-quality chert. The mining activity appears to be a later phenomenon not directly related to Early Woodland ritual use (Crothers 2012).

Sources: Munson and Munson 1990; Crothers 2012

◉ Newt Kash Shelter, Kentucky

Terminal Archaic women's retreat • Menifee County

Location: Menifee County, eastern Kentucky (37.920°N, 84.820°W)

A Terminal Archaic rockshelter that Claassen (2011, American Antiquity 76:628-641) interprets as a women's retreat — a place of menstrual seclusion, birthing, and healing. The evidence includes: highly regulated, ritualized spacing of features; material correlates for women's activities (nutting stones, cordage debris, bedrock mortars); and patterns consistent with Claassen's broader argument that many Terminal Archaic shelters in the Eastern U.S. served as ritualistic retreats where women conducted activities related to birth, healing, and death. Homsey-Messer (2015) cites this site as evidence for the gendered coupling of caves with ritual function.

Sources: Claassen 2011 (American Antiquity 76:628-641); Homsey-Messer 2015; Webb & Funkhouser 1936

◉ Meadowcroft Rockshelter

National Historic Landmark • Possibly Oldest Site of Human Habitation in North America

Location: Cross Creek drainage, Washington County, SW Pennsylvania (40.284°N, 80.013°W)

A deeply stratified sandstone rockshelter that may contain the oldest evidence of human habitation in North America. Excavated by James Adovasio (University of Pittsburgh) from 1973–1978, with 11 major strata spanning over 16,000 years. The deepest radiocarbon dates (~19,000–16,000 B.P.) are among the most debated in American archaeology — if valid, they push back human presence in the Eastern Woodlands by several thousand years before Clovis. Even skeptics accept the middle strata as documenting continuous habitation from ~12,000 B.P. onward. More than 20,000 artifacts recovered including the oldest known basketry in North America (~13,000 B.P.). The shelter is formed by a sandstone overhang ~15 m wide and ~6 m deep along Cross Creek, a tributary of the Ohio River. The site's stratigraphy is exceptionally clear, with distinct cultural horizons separated by sterile deposits.

Today: Meadowcroft Rockshelter and Historic Village (Avella, PA). Open to public as a museum operated by the Senator John Heinz History Center. National Historic Landmark.

Sources: Adovasio et al. 1990 (American Antiquity 55:348-354); Adovasio et al. 1998; Goldberg & Arpin 1999

◉ Graham Cave

National Historic Landmark • ~10,000 B.P. onward • Dalton through Woodland

Location: Loutre River, Montgomery County, Missouri (38.902°N, 91.833°W)

A dolomite rockshelter on the Loutre River, a tributary of the Missouri. One of the earliest excavated stratified Archaic sites in the Midwest, with Dalton culture occupation dating to ~10,000 B.P. Excavated by Wilfred Logan (1949–1955) for the Missouri Archaeological Society. The shelter is 30 m wide and 5 m deep, facing south along the river. Cultural deposits reach ~2.5 m deep, with Dalton, Early Archaic, Middle Archaic, Late Archaic, and Woodland components. Key artifacts include a Dalton cemetery, bone tools, deer antler flakers, and early side-notched points. Graham Cave State Park since 1964.

Today: Graham Cave State Park (MO DNR). Shelter visible from trail. Interpretive signage.

Sources: Logan 1952; Chapman 1975; MO DNR

◉ Rodgers Shelter

Multi-component Archaic through Woodland • Pomme de Terre River

Location: Pomme de Terre River valley, Benton County, Missouri (37.925°N, 93.403°W)

A deeply stratified rockshelter in the Western Ozarks, excavated as salvage archaeology ahead of the Truman Reservoir project (1963–1968, University of Missouri). Over 7 m of cultural deposits spanning the Early Archaic through Late Woodland periods. Key for understanding the Western Ozark Archaic sequence — particularly the Dalton through Middle Archaic transition. Faunal and botanical remains document the shift from deer-focused hunting to a diversified economy incorporating nut processing and early plant cultivation. The shelter is formed by a sandstone overhang along a tributary of the Pomme de Terre River. Now under the waters of Truman Reservoir.

Sources: Wood & McMillan 1976; Ahler 1971; Kay 1982

◉ Jaguar Cave, Tennessee

Early cave exploration — mixed male/female • Fentress County

Location: Fentress County, Tennessee (36.230°N, 85.605°W)

Among the early cave exploration sites in the Eastern U.S., with evidence spanning ~5,000 to ~2,500 years ago — predating the intensive Early Woodland mineral mining at Mammoth/Salts. The natural entrance is through a stream passage and much of the cave is too wet for gypsum formation. Gypsum present in drier portions shows no evidence of mining. Crothers (2012) notes that Claassen (2001, 2011) suggested caves were the exclusive domain of men, but analysis of prehistoric footprints in Jaguar Cave showed that early exploration parties were composed of men and women (Watson et al. 2005:38) — a distinctly different pattern from the exclusively male Early Woodland period use.

Sources: Watson et al. 2005; Crothers 2012; Crothers et al. 2002
